title: Floer theory in spaces of stable pairs over Riemann surfaces
speaker: Timothy Perutz
affiliation: University of Texas, Austin; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will report on joint work with Andrew Lee, which explores the notion that spaces of stable pairs over Riemann surfaces (in the sense of Bradlow and Thaddeus) could form a natural home for a “non-abelian” analog of Heegaard Floer homology for 3-manifolds - just as the g-fold symmetric product is the home of Heegaard Floer homology - thereby circumventing the problems with singularities that beset instanton-type theories.

proof_stages.label: σ-stable pairs and fixed-determinant moduli
proof_stages.summary: The lecture defines rank-two holomorphic pairs (E,φ), the two σ-stability inequalities for line subbundles, and the fixed-determinant moduli spaces M^λ_σ, which are smooth compact Kähler manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Gauge-theoretic motivation for stable-pair spaces
proof_stages.summary: Stable-pair moduli are proposed as smooth Floer-theoretic replacements for singular even-degree moduli of projectively flat connections, motivated by dimensional reductions of instanton, Seiberg-Witten, and non-abelian Seiberg-Witten equations.

proof_stages.label: Thaddeus chamber structure and the top chamber
proof_stages.summary: Thaddeus' variation of σ is reviewed: the nonempty chambers M_i begin with a projective space and a blowup, successive chambers differ by flips, and the final small-σ chamber M_top maps by an Abel-Jacobi-type map to the moduli of semistable bundles.

proof_stages.label: Fano target for non-abelian Heegaard Floer theory
proof_stages.summary: The preferred stable-pair space is chosen to be the top chamber with degree d=2g+2, giving a monotone/Fano 3g-dimensional manifold M_Z and leading to the conjecture that handlebodies define embedded Lagrangians diffeomorphic to (S^3)^g.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-point Floer package for stable-pair monodromy
proof_stages.summary: For surface bundles over S^1 the talk recalls the mapping-torus formulation of symplectic monodromy and fixed-point Floer homology, constructs the extended mapping-class-group action on stable-pair moduli, and defines stable-pair Floer homology HSP as the resulting fixed-point Floer homology.

proof_stages.label: Genus-one stable-pair Floer computation
proof_stages.summary: When Z is elliptic, M_Z is the blowup of CP^3 along an elliptic quartic; its quantum cohomology eigensplitting isolates a -1 summand, and under homological nondegeneracy HSP_{-1} is proved isomorphic to fixed-point Floer homology of φ acting on Z, with an additional even Z^4 summand.

proof_stages.label: Proof via explicit blowup model and continuation
proof_stages.summary: Because Lagrangian-correspondence methods are obstructed in genus one, the computation uses an explicit model mimicking Morse theory on codimension-two blowups to control fixed points, then applies Lee-Usher continuation principles to pass from low-weight blowup forms to the anticanonical form.

proof_stages.label: Higher-genus outlook and handlebody Lagrangians
proof_stages.summary: The final flip locus in degree 2g+2 contains a projective bundle over Sym^g(Z), suggesting symmetric-product Floer summands in higher genus and identifying the construction of handlebody Lagrangians and stabilization invariance as the next major steps.

examples.name: Heegaard-Floer symmetric products and Heegaard tori
examples.note: The standard construction of Lagrangian tori in Sym^g(Z) from handlebodies is used as the visible analogy that motivates choosing degree d=2g+2 in the rank-two stable-pair setting.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains why the Heegaard-Floer choice is Sym^g and explicitly constructs the Heegaard tori as points with one point on each bounding circle, noting the resulting locus is Lagrangian.

passes.reason: Standard symmetric products and Heegaard tori are used as the motivating Heegaard-Floer model, despite the word “symmetric.”
standard symmetric products and Heegaard tori are used as the ordinary Heegaard-Floer model, not to refute a claim
Sym^g of a surface with the usual Heegaard tori is used as the standard Heegaard-Floer model and analogy, not as a structurally sporadic or refuting case.
The Sym^g surface and its Heegaard product tori are used as the standard Heegaard-Floer model, not as maximally symmetric or as a refutation.
the degree d=g symmetric product is the critical dimension-matching case in which the Heegaard product tori are Lagrangian, used as a model
the Heegaard-Floer model uses the critical degree Sym^g where the handlebody torus has exactly Lagrangian dimension
Used as a positive motivating model, with Sym^g and the Heegaard tori at the dimension-critical choice d=g that makes the tori Lagrangian.
Used as the standard Heegaard-Floer model motivating the degree choice, with ordinary symmetric products and product Heegaard tori.
Used as the motivating Heegaard-Floer model, with d=g the critical degree making the handlebody tori Lagrangian.
Used as the motivating Heegaard-Floer model, with the choice Sym^g and Heegaard tori critical for producing Lagrangians rather than refuting a claim.
standard symmetric-product Heegaard-Floer geometry and its Heegaard tori are used as a motivating analogue, not as a failure or special classification exception
Sym^g of a genus-g Heegaard surface is used as the critical-degree Heegaard-Floer model where the handlebody tori become Lagrangian, not as a disproof.

examples.name: Genus-one handlebody Lagrangian 3-spheres
examples.note: For genus one the conjectural handlebody Lagrangian is known: it is a Lagrangian 3-sphere obtained as a vanishing cycle in a Lefschetz pencil, illustrating the broader handlebody-Lagrangian conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: In the genus-one case he computes the relevant degree as 4, identifies M_Z as the blow-up of CP^3 along the elliptic curve, and says the handlebody Lagrangian 3-spheres are constructed as vanishing cycles of a Lefschetz pencil.

passes.reason: The genus-one handlebody case is the minimal nontrivial base case, where the expected product of Lagrangian 3-spheres reduces to a single sphere and verifies the conjecture.
the genus-one case is the minimal base case where the expected product of Lagrangian 3-spheres reduces to a single S^3 and verifies the conjectural picture
The genus-one case is the minimal nontrivial handlebody case, where the expected product of g three-spheres reduces to a single Lagrangian S^3 and verifies rather than refutes the conjecture.
The genus-one case is the minimal base case where the predicted product of g 3-spheres reduces to a single Lagrangian S^3 and is used as a positive verification.
the genus-one case is the minimal base case where the predicted product of g three-spheres reduces to a single vanishing-cycle S^3
the genus-one case is the minimal base case of the proposed product-of-3-spheres Lagrangian construction
Used as the verified genus-one base case, a minimal nontrivial handlebody case giving a single Lagrangian S^3 rather than a higher product.
Used as the verified minimal genus-one base case, where the conjectural product of Lagrangian 3-spheres reduces to a single sphere.
Used as the verified minimal-genus base case of the Lagrangian-handlebody conjecture, not to refute anything.
Presented as the genus-one base case where the conjectural handlebody Lagrangian exists, not as a failure case.
the genus-one Lagrangian 3-sphere case is the minimal/base case of the handlebody conjecture and is used as a positive construction
The genus-one construction is the minimal positive-genus base case of the handlebody Lagrangian-sphere conjecture and is presented as a verified case.

examples.name: Symmetric-product fixed-point Floer homology for fibered 3-manifolds
examples.note: The known rank-one/symmetric-product correspondence with a summand of monopole Floer homology is presented as the precedent that suggests studying fixed-point Floer homology for monodromy acting on stable-pair spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes symmetric-product fixed-point Floer homology for fibered 3-manifolds as a known gauge-theoretic model and motivation, without computing it for a specific symmetric product or monodromy.

passes.reason: Symmetric-product fixed-point Floer homology for a fibered 3-manifold is cited as a standard systematic gauge-theoretic analogue.
symmetric-product fixed-point Floer homology is cited as a standard precedent for fibered 3-manifolds

Cited as an established positive analogue for fibered 3-manifolds, with ordinary Sym^d fixed-point Floer homology for general d.
Used as a standard precedent relating symmetric-product fixed-point Floer homology to monopole Floer homology for fibered 3-manifolds.
Used as a standard precedent relating fixed-point Floer homology of symmetric products to monopole Floer theory.
Cited as a standard systematic fixed-point Floer theory for monodromy on symmetric products giving a gauge-theoretic summand.
fixed-point Floer homology on symmetric products for fibered 3-manifolds is cited as a standard model with gauge-theoretic interpretation
Fixed-point Floer homology for monodromy on symmetric products is a standard motivating analogue with a gauge-theoretic interpretation.

examples.name: Mirror-symmetry check of quantum eigenvalues
examples.note: The Hori–Vafa–Givental mirror superpotential is mentioned only as an independent way to recover the quantum-cohomology eigenvalues already computed directly.
examples.engagement_reason: He states that the directly computed quantum eigenvalues agree with those obtained from the Hori-Vafa/Givental mirror superpotential, but does not carry out the mirror calculation.

passes.reason: The mirror-symmetry calculation is used as a consistency check for ordinary quantum-cohomology eigenvalues, not as a failure case.
the mirror-symmetry computation is a consistency check for ordinary quantum-cohomology eigenvalues, not an obstruction or sporadic object
The mirror-superpotential computation merely agrees with the directly computed quantum c1 eigenvalues, serving as a consistency check rather than a counterexample.

examples.name: Ivan Smith's higher-genus Lagrangian-correspondence comparison
examples.note: Smith's result for blowups along intersections of two quadrics is used as a nearby comparison showing why an attractive Fukaya-categorical route does not directly prove the genus-one theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He summarizes Ivan Smith’s higher-genus blow-up and Lagrangian-correspondence result and its limitation to genus greater than one, without working through the comparison.

passes.reason: Smith’s higher-genus Lagrangian-correspondence result is a systematic family cited for comparison, with its genus-one limitation noted but not itself serving as a counterexample.
